title: Toad in the Hole
subtitle: Yorkshire pudding batter around sausages, served with onion gravy
excerpt: Toad in the hole is Yorkshire pudding batter poured around browned sausages in a very hot tin. The batter puffs up and sets around the sausages, giving a crisp outside and a soft, eggy interior. The onion gravy that goes alongside is not optional: the richness of the sausages and the eggy batter need something sharp and savoury to cut through them.
sourceNotes: Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: sausage in batter pudding method. Traditional British family supper dish with documented history from the early eighteenth century.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make the batter up to 24 hours ahead and refrigerate. Cold batter poured into screaming-hot fat rises just as well as room-temperature batter.

glossaryTerms.definition: The principle that batter dishes such as toad in the hole and Yorkshire puddings must be poured into fat that is already shimmering and beginning to smoke. The rapid contact between cold batter and smoking-hot fat creates immediate steam, which puffs the batter before it sets.

content.text: The name is curious and unexplained. The dish is, at its most literal, sausages in batter, but that description understates what happens to the batter when it goes into a

tin: it puffs, crisps at the edges, and sets into a custardy interior that is quite different from the dense batter of a thick pancake. The sausages provide the fat that keeps the batter from sticking and adds flavour as they render.

The method mirrors Yorkshire puddings: make a rested batter, heat the fat until smoking, add the batter quickly, and do not open the oven for the first 20 minutes. The difference is that the sausages are browned in the tin first, and their rendered fat becomes the cooking fat for the batter.
A roasting tin large enough that the sausages sit in a single layer with room for the batter to rise around them. A tin that is too small crowds the sausages and the batter steams rather than rises.

Sift {{plain-flour}} of plain flour and {{sea-salt-fine}} into a large bowl. Make a well, add the 4 {{eggs}} eggs, and whisk until smooth. Gradually beat in {{whole-milk}} of whole milk to make a smooth, fluid batter. Rest for at least 30 minutes.

Heat the oven to 230°C (210°C fan). Pour {{sunflower-oil}} of sunflower oil into the roasting tin. Add the 8 {{sausages-pork}} sausages in a single layer. Roast for 15 minutes until the sausages are golden-brown on the outside and some fat has rendered into the oil. The combined fat in the tin should be shimmering and beginning to smoke.

Pull the tin from the oven and quickly pour the batter around the sausages (not over them). The batter should sizzle immediately. Return to the oven immediately. Bake for 25 to 30 minutes without opening the door. The batter is done when it has risen well above the rim of the tin and is deeply golden-brown at the edges. Do not open the door before 25 minutes.

rows.cause: Fat not hot enough when batter was poured in
rows.fix: The fat in the tin with the browned sausages must be visibly smoking. If it is not, return the tin to the oven for a further 5 minutes.

rows.cause: Too much batter for the tin size, or oven temperature dropped
rows.fix: Use a larger tin. The batter should be at most 2 cm deep before rising.

rows.cause: Oven not fully preheated, or too much space between sausages
rows.fix: Preheat the tin and oil together before adding sausages. Turn the sausages once during the browning phase.

content.text: Toad in the hole appears in British cookery books from at least the early eighteenth century, where it described various meats baked in batter rather than specifically sausages. The sausage version became standard by the Victorian era. Mrs Beeton's recipe calls for small pieces of beef dripping in the batter, which is now generally omitted. The dish sits firmly in the family supper category: it is cheap, filling, and universally liked. Serve with onion gravy and buttered mashed potato.
